title: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
chief_complaint: My father has become forgetful, he's walking like his feet are stuck to the floor, and he can't control his bladder anymore. This started over the past 6 months. The walking problems started first, then the memory, then the incontinence.

physical_exam.Neurologic: Gait: Magnetic gait (feet appear stuck to floor). Wide-based, short shuffling steps. Turns are multiple small steps (en bloc turning). Postural instability. No tremor. No rigidity. Cognitive: MMSE 22/30. Impaired executive function, attention, and recall. Frontal release signs (grasp reflex positive).
physical_exam.Urinary: Incontinent of urine. No spinal tenderness.

hidden_data.mri_brain: Ventriculomegaly (Evans index >0.3) out of proportion to sulcal atrophy. DESH (Disproportionately Enlarged Subarachnoid space Hydrocephalus). Flow void in cerebral aqueduct on T2 (increased CSF flow). No obstruction.
hidden_data.lumbar_puncture: Opening pressure 14 cmH2O (NORMAL). Large volume LP (40mL CSF removed): Gait and cognitive testing 2 hours post-LP: Gait speed improves 40%. MMSE improves from 22 to 26.

correct_diagnosis: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH) - Hakim-Adams Triad, DESH Pattern on MRI, Positive Response to LP
key_learning_points: NPH = Reversible cause of dementia! Classic triad (Hakim-Adams): Gait disturbance (FIRST and most prominent), Dementia (subcortical type - frontal lobe dysfunction), Urinary incontinence (urge type). Occurs in this order: Gait → Cognition → Incontinence
Diagnosis: Ventriculomegaly on imaging + Normal LP opening pressure + Clinical improvement after large-volume LP (CSF drainage test)
MRI: DESH pattern - ventriculomegaly with tight high-convexity sulci. Evans index >0.3. Aqueductal flow void

Treatment: Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt. Best response if gait predominant, short duration (<2 years), known cause, and positive response to CSF drainage. Gait improves most, then cognition, then incontinence (last to improve, first to return)
Differentiate from Alzheimer (memory first, then gait), Parkinson (tremor, rigidity, asymmetric onset, levodopa response), and vascular dementia (stepwise decline, vascular risk factors)
